speaker: interviewee
text: And that time, a number of people were shot. Because they ventured not so much outside. Nobody was allowed outside. But there were some inner courtyards where they had walks in a-- within the buildings.

speaker: interviewer
text: So a woman across the street was shot. I don't know who-- what member of my family-- came to the window. And they fired a bullet that came through the window and it hit-- lodged in the ceiling. So this is when things began to be sort of hitting home.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because the schools were closed. Soon afterwards, the arm bands-- everybody had to where an arm band. I was too young to wear an armband. So I took advantage of it. And I was running around. And I looked-- I didn't look Jewish. So I would go places where Jewish-- where Jews were forbidden. I would even go places which were only for Germans. And I would go right along there. I mean, the-- I remember there was a big celebration. The center of the town was changed. The name was changed to Adolf Hitler Platz. There was a big ceremony. And I went to that. And my mother would let me. I mean, she had confidence that I knew what I was doing. And they began to play the German national anthem, all the people around me had stood with their hands out, so I did, too. And then the Governor Frank came down with his limousine. And I ran to the car. I mean, this kind of thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was still an adventure. And I enjoyed myself. And I felt totally free. It were-- whenever somebody wanted to do something which was-- Jews could not do, I managed to do this. Then a lot of it on, when it really was dangerous. So there I had no sense of fear. I felt so self-confident. And it worked, too. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So then came the-- my father lost his business.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans would usually appoint somebody else, a German, to take over. And they kicked him out. I don't know the details. This is something-- my parents usually discussed things, so I didn't hear them. I was very often-- they sort of kept secrets from me in order not to upset me. And I didn't probe. So very often, I didn't quite know what was going on. But I knew the business was taken away. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The ghetto was going to be set up in the beginning of 1941. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Well, when the time came, only people who had certain skills or professions or who were useful to the Germans received the permit to live in the ghetto. And the others had to leave town.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I'm skipping maybe a whole year at this point. However, so in March of 1941, we moved to a little village about 20 miles outside of Krakow where we lived with a Polish peasant. They had a small hut with two rooms. And we had sort of part of one room. And this was for my parents, my-- my grandmother, my brother, and one of my uncles. My sister was, at that time, in a [INAUDIBLE] of another town. So what town-- was the name of the town you were in?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a small village called Nieznanowice [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was the name. It's primitive as the name of the village. Nieznanowice means-- it means unknown or a place which is unknown. And it was until this day. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] this was in March. I concentrated at that point to become bar mitzvah, which was next month. So I was 13 in April of 1941. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I remember that I guess they had to search the neighborhood to find 10 Jews, 10 men. But I guess it was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] possible. So I had sort of a bar mitzvah.

speaker: interviewer
text: The people you were staying with were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, they were not?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they're Poles.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was their attitude towards your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Polite hostility. In other words, they were not particularly friendly. However, they were-- they were getting paid. And we would help them out. I was working a lot in the fields with them, all kinds of-- this was a family that owned land. And so they had a cow and-- and three acres of land. And I would always work with them. And they would make me work hard. But I enjoyed it. It was sort of a kind of life that was quiet. Nobody bothered us. [INAUDIBLE] was quite peaceful.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Until August or July of '42. Now, during this time, I had an uncle who lived in Krakow. We heard there was some action took place in Krakow. I was the one who would go to Krakow [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and sneak my way into the ghetto. And I'd done it several times through the wires, under the wires. And I went to search for him. He was gone. But I remember, I went there-- this is the-- the mind-- I would go to the ghetto. And then one afternoon I would sneak out through the wire, go to the movie in town, and sneak back in again. So this was, to me, perfectly normal.

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean, the police was chasing me. And I was laughing at them. I mean, who was going to catch me? And I used to travel to town, often, to see my sister, whenever there were some special things. And I would use German vehicles to-- to stop the ministry's and travel this way.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was, to me, like-- that was all right. So I was doing this a lot when we were living in the village. And then in 1941, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] an order came in for all men over 21, between 21 and 55, or something like this, to report to a certain town. I forgot what happened. But I came across a postcard that I wrote to my sister that she preserved. And I'm saying in it that the-- the men didn't go. They sent a letter instead to send them a pass to travel to this town. Because without the pass, they were not allowed to travel. So this way they avoided going. However, the next year, 1942, they were told to go again. And they went and never came back. They were taken to a labor camp in Krakow, called Rakowice, which was at the airport.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this left-- my brother and my uncle went. And this left just me and my father, who was getting sick with stomach problems-- I don't know whether it was also a cancer, I don't know-- and my mother and my grandmother. And things were getting very difficult.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were after me to report to the labor camp. But somehow they have arranged with the local authorities that I didn't go. I don't know exactly how it was done. But things were getting bad. And in the meantime, they were beginning to do more and more of the cleaning up of towns and villages of Jews. And we knew that our time would be coming. So as a matter of fact, my sister was sending us warnings to that effect. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So then what has happened, about July-- I'm sorry--

speaker: interviewer
text: I was just curious, did you think of leaving? Did you consider fleeing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the idea was this, that if-- you see, in 1942-- or not, '41 rather, the war with Russia began. And the possibility existed for a while that maybe the war will be cut short. Maybe we can hide someplace. Maybe some friendly Poles will take us in.

speaker: interviewer
text: We didn't know. The-- the question was if we report that way we were supposed to report, the chances of being killed were very high. So what kind of risk do you take? And how do you-- how do you go about this?

speaker: interviewee
text: What has happened-- this is sort of a critical story in my life. We received orders-- all the Jews in the village, there were three families-- to report to Bochnia for transport, for resettlement, as they called it. It was in about a week or 10 days whatever it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we prepared ourselves to do that, because we had no choice. What were we going to do? We can't stay there. We had no place to go. So we were resigning ourselves we're going to go and thinking of packing some warm clothing. They'll probably take us to the East. I mean this was hoping against hope that maybe this is a real resettlement. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: This was supervised by the Polish police, which was in the neighboring little town about 3 miles away. So the Polish policeman was assigned to sort of look on us every once in a while to make sure that we are there, didn't run away or anything like this. And he was the type of a guy-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] made himself appear friendly. So I remember my father invited him in one day to sit down and maybe have a schnapps. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And so I was sitting around the table with them. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Might want to close the window. That's all [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. The-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we're sitting around the table. And he was very friendly-like. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And at one point, he wrote a little note which he put-- left on the table. He says, let P. Kempler-- P is an abbreviation which could be [NON-ENGLISH] or [NON-ENGLISH], Mr. or Mrs.-- come to see me tomorrow at 1 o'clock at my home.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I remember seeing that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] note. And it struck me. You know? I mean, what does he-- I almost said, well, it doesn't say who. But I didn't say anything. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember later on, I was walking-- my mother took me to some Polish people who were friendly. She was talking to them about possibly taking me in for a period of time until the war ends. Because the people wouldn't think about it. We tried two different ones. And I remember, because we were walking back. It was sort of dark. And I was holding her hand. And since I was the know-it-all, I was telling her how we shouldn't be afraid to be shot. And I was describing to her exactly how it was going to be. But I was ready to die with her. I mean, she didn't say anything. I mean, she knew me that I knew all the answers. But-- but my feelings were [INAUDIBLE] that if we die, we die together. I mean, we don't worry about the fact that we have not found any place to stay. The next day, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my mother got dressed. She was wearing her best dress, I remember, a blue with white polka dots and lipstick. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And she didn't say anything. And she left. She went to the policeman. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was 14 and really naive. I mean, yes, I knew, and I didn't, this kind of a thing. But when she left, there was a meadow outside of the hut. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I laid in the meadow. I didn't stay in the house. She left. I saw her going, but I didn't [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] say anything to her. And I was just lying there, maybe three or four hours. That's at least my best recollection. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this was a beautiful summer day, sort of a great day to be alive. Looking at this later on, I always said to myself-- I kept some journals and did some writing about these things. I refer to this as the day I said goodbye to life, which is partly true, not quite. The fact is that, you know, there were birds singing. And I was lying in the grass. And all the insects were buzzing. And it was just a lovely day. And I heard the voice of the kids playing in the background. And I felt very strongly that-- that I'm going to die. It was a terrible feeling, which I never allowed myself before to feel this way. Except in this case, it was intensified by the fact that my mother went to that policeman. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I was lying there. And this is when something broke in me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So whatever has happened, has affected my life. This is when something broke emotionally. I shut down in some way. I don't know exactly what actually has happened. But this is the day, the event, that sort of changed me for good. When I got up from that, my mother was coming. My father was in the hut by himself. I wouldn't want to go and see him When she came back and I got up from the meadow, I was a different person. I was-- something has broken is, I guess, the best way to describe it-- describe it. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I guess whatever happened there was denied even though what has happened the next day, the policeman did not show up. This was a Saturday. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were packing to go, but we talked to a next door neighbor. And he would arrange for him that he was going to take us after midnight. He would take us across the river to a forest. And we were going to run away, take advantage of the fact that the policeman was not there. And I guess the policeman was not there, because my mother bought him off. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Only it wasn't money. So we took off to the woods, my father, my mother, and my grandmother. I was the-- I became the leader. I knew the-- you know, I was the know-it-all again. And I knew how to-- with the question of where to go, how to make sure that we find a place where there was water-- water, not to make noise, not to have-- to have the wind blowing in such a way that-- whatever, the various conditions of surviving in the woods. I would sneak at night to steal fruit from-- off the trees, approaching from such a way that the dogs would not smell-- smell me.

speaker: interviewer
text: I knew all of these adventure stories not to do that. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But it was-- this was not the way to be. Something had to go. How long can we stay in the woods? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, apparently, what has happened that time in the meadow changed my feeling towards my mother. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Before, I was willing to die with her. And now, as I-- going back many years later, I guess the feelings were somehow she betrayed me. At the same time, she saved my life. And I decided one day in the woods, totally without any preconceived ideas, I'm leaving. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I was-- I told them that there were too much-- many of us, rather. Our chances of survival are limited. I'm going to go to Krakow on my own. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And my mother tried to stop me, says that I bring them courage. But whatever it was, I decided, no, I'm going. And she, in her wisdom-- and it was always my mother. The father and my grandmother like didn't exist. In her wisdom, she decided that maybe this-- there's a chance. So this was the first time I put my long pants on, my father's pants. Up to that time, I never had long pants. She shaved my upper lip that got some fuzz on it to make sure this doesn't look Jewish. And I took off leaving them in the woods. Now, when I left, the idea was I am not going to see them again. But I didn't care. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I have this change began to manifest itself in this way. I ran away from them. Strangely enough, when I left the woods and I walked down the streets in the village, I had sort of a rucksack with some piece of bread and cheese in it and a little bit of money. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I felt free. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was a strange sense of freedom. Because the responsibility for them, it-- it was-- it weighed on me so heavy. I can not-- I do not want to be responsible for the lives of others. I mean, I didn't know at the time what it was. This all came back later. But at that time, I felt free. Now, the way to go to Krakow was to stand on a busy corner and hail German trucks, who occasionally would stop and take people. And sometimes it took many hours. So I stood there. And all of a sudden, I see the-- the boy, who was part of the peasant family we were in, was going down the street. And he saw me. And he ran back. And then a few minutes later, the old man where we lived wearing his Sunday suit, is going down-- he was going to go, obviously, to report me to the police. At that time, if you reported a Jew, you would get 5 kilos of sugar which was quite a reward. So I was waiting. Then I said, well, I have to allow so much time. This was-- the potato fields were pretty high. I was going to find my way and hide in the potato fields for the night. And just then, the-- a German truck stopped. In his view, he was coming over there. And I waved to him. And-- and I gave him the [INAUDIBLE]. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I went to Krakow. There were many, many adventures. I mean, getting to Krakow, the-- the-- the access to Krakow was always blocked on the main streets, primarily because people coming from the villages usually were black marketeers who were bringing food and stuff into town. So they inspected everybody and so on. I mean, I had ways of getting through. I mean, I was perfect at that. Anyway, I went in Krakow. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had no place to stay. I had some people, some Poles I knew. But they wouldn't take me in.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I would sleep in-- like, in the house where we lived in way on the top there is a floor which leads to the attic where people used to hang their laundry. So I would sleep on top of there or find places, where courtyards, boxes, crates, whatever there was.

speaker: interviewee
text: And during the day, I would read books and go to the movies. Total-- I mean, this was a way of-- totally unreasonable. But I was thinking only of the immediate. I remember sometimes go to-- went to the movies. By the time I came out, I didn't know where I was going to go to sleep. I didn't have my-- I had to get rid of my rucksack with the bread and cheese. Because I couldn't carry this around. I didn't eat for days. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But-- and then I was trying to make contact. Because I saw Jewish people were coming in from the ghetto to work in town. And I got in touch with them. The-- Anita, my girlfriend, was in the ghetto. Her uncle was a German Jew who was very influential with the German for some reason. And he had a high position with the Germans. I mean, he was really-- whatever he wanted, he could get. Anyway, I finally wound that I worked myself into the ghetto, illegally, of course. So even there, there were people who were escaping, like me, from labor camps and so on. So there were daily raids. I got away, but my goal was I couldn't stay in the ghetto either, even though during the day I would spend time with her. We were always playing games, playing school, playing cards. I mean, the total non-reality of that. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There was a group of people who were working in the labor camp where my brother was. My brother was in a camp. And these people from the ghetto used to go there for the day. They were taken by truck, and then come back in the evening. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one day, I worked myself on a truck with them, went to the-- the camp. And I found out what the-- the group is that-- that-- where my brother was assigned to. And I appeared there. And I started working. And so this was run by civilians, incidentally.

speaker: interviewee
text: The guy came in and says, who are you. And I said, I'm the brother of [INAUDIBLE] Kempler. And I came to be with him. So I volunteered.

speaker: interviewer
text: He said, how old are you? Well, almost 15. All right. So all of a sudden, I was a member of the camp. And this was my salvation, because I was safe. And I was there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, I had an identity and some place I could sleep and some place I could live. My brother was working as a master tailor. He had a wonderful thing. All the Germans, all the German officers from all around were having uniforms made by him for their wives and so on, down the line. I was working very, very hard. It was very difficult. But I didn't mind.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what kind of work were you doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Construction. These people building built construction, like buildings, hospitals, and various kind of things of this [INAUDIBLE]. So it was construction. Hard work. I mean, I remember having to unload the cement which are 50 kilogram bags, too heavy for me. I would-- they would throw them on my back, and I had run with it. I would collapse. Because I was still too-- too weak to carry this kind of a bag. It was tough.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your brother react you're just showing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother was-- I don't know that he really wanted me. I became maybe a burden. He had his own soft spot in there. I-- so we were remote. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I always was sort of [INAUDIBLE] with him I guess. In the mean time, I thought my parents were dead. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They didn't die. They left the woods. And they made their way to Bochnia. In the meantime, Bochnia was the head of resettlement. But some Jews-- they hadn't clean the ghetto yet. There were a number of Jews left. And they settled in there. They survived, all three of them. And finally, through my sister, and there was still mail going on, we realized that all of us are alive. So in spite of the fact that I left them, and given them-- I've given them up entirely for dead. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They were alive, but I had no feelings for them at that point. Interesting, when the first winter of 1942 approached, most of the people who were brought to the camp were brought in the summer time. They had no winter clothing or anything like this. So the-- the camp civilians had arranged that they would send the people to the place where they came from, wherever they had possessions, to bring some winter clothing. And I went with my brother and my uncle to the village. But first of all, we stopped in Bochnia to see my parents. Then we went to the village. We had some adventures there, too. We were arrested and all kinds of things. But finally, we got a lot of our possessions. Not only for ourselves, but also for things that were-- then we, again, went to the parents and left them there. Now, at that point, as I said, the camp was not run by the military. I could have escaped and stayed there. I didn't want to, because I said I can-- my-- I have a better chance of surviving in the camp than you are, where you were in a ghetto. And this was ghetto B. This was the part of the ghetto which was usually older people or sick people, who don't work. They usually were the first victims. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then another time, they used to have a truck going from the camp to a forest to bring lumber in for construction.
